title: Gauging the interplay between market liquidity and funding liquidity
summary: This ECB Financial Stability Review special feature analyzes the relationship between market liquidity and funding liquidity, developing composite indicators to assess how these two dimensions interact and potentially generate liquidity spirals in the euro area. The authors note that as monetary policy normalizes, cyclical factors have shifted toward more restrictive liquidity conditions, with heightened bond market volatility and increased uncertainty about policy path.
key_points: Market liquidity and funding liquidity are inherently connected and can reinforce each other to generate liquidity spirals
Market liquidity has multiple dimensions: immediacy (pace of trade execution), breadth and depth (scope and size of transactions), tightness (cost and price impact), and resilience
Funding liquidity risks include rollover risk (inability to replace short-term funding), redemption risk (depositor withdrawals), and haircut/margining risk
Cyclical determinants affecting liquidity include investor risk appetite, macroeconomic conditions, and global monetary policy cycles
Bond market volatility has increased due to subdued free float of high-quality debt and uncertainty about monetary policy path
Data challenges exist due to sparse corporate bond market data, fragmented OTC trading, and gaps in data for non-bank financial intermediaries
why_it_matters: Understanding the interplay between market and funding liquidity is critical for financial stability as these dimensions can reinforce each other during stress events, potentially leading to systemic crises. Market participants, regulators, and risk managers need to monitor these indicators to anticipate liquidity regime shifts that could amplify market dislocations.
market_structure_context: The article directly addresses market structure by examining market-making practices, trade transparency, and the resilience of market-makers during stress periods. It notes that the gradual automation of trading, including high-frequency trading, may undermine the robustness of individual liquidity measures, and that limited data on algorithmic trading makes proper assessment challenging.
notes: Historical record (published May 2023). Source body was truncated but sufficient content available for analysis. Authors: Nander de Vette, Benjamin Klaus, Simon Kördel, and Andrzej Sowiński. Part of ECB Financial Stability Review, May 2023. High-quality institutional research with authoritative ECB branding.
